title: RA.1001 Adrian Sherwood
tracklist:
African Head Charge - Asalatua
Adrian Sherwood - Russian Oscillator 
Coldcut & Adrian Sherwood - Metro
Dub Syndicate - Pleasurezone Transmitter 
Sonic Boom & Panda Bear - In My Body Dub 
Horace Andy & Lone Ranger - Dub Guidance
Creation Rebel - Jubilee Clock 
Neysatu & Likkle Mai - War
Lee "Scratch" Perry & Vin Gordon - Crickets In Moonlight
Adrian Sherwood & Friends - On-U In Space 
Los Gaiteros de San Jacinto - Fuego de Cumbia / Dub de Sangre Pura - Dub Mix
Audio Active feat. Bim Sherman - Free the Marijuana
Spoon - The Devil & Mister Jones - Adrian Sherwood Remix 
New Age Steppers - Aggro Dub Version 
Sherwood & Pinch - Excessive Drinking 
Higher Authorities - Neptune - Version 
Mark Stewart - Favour 
Adrian Sherwood - The Collapse Of Everything 
African Head Charge - I Chant Too

translation.content: Adrian Sherwood has spent nearly half a century reshaping how dub is heard, felt and understood. From his teenage years absorbing reggae and funk at the Newlands Club in High Wycombe, to cofounding his revered On-U Sound label in 1979, he's been a restless force in British sound system culture. His debut production, Dub From Creation, was striking for what it wasn't—instead, it signalled his instinct to twist the Jamaican genre into new, experimental forms. That invention defined On-U Sound. The outlet epitomised the creative collision of reggae and sound system music with post-punk, industrial, synth pop and beyond, forging a catalogue that still sounds singular today. In the process, he became a vital bridge: producing for the likes of Prince Far I, Bim Sherman and Lee "Scratch" Perry while also working with Depeche Mode, Nine Inch Nails and Sinead O'Connor. For some, it was radical—"What on earth are you doing to reggae?" David Rodigan once asked after listening to African Head Charge—but for Sherwood, nostalgia was the real death knell. His mantra was simple: don't follow, make your own sound. That ethos runs straight through The Collapse Of Everything, his first solo album in 13 years. It’s a heavy, hallucinatory record that draws on desert blues riffs, dread bass pressure and noirish textures from collaborators like Doug Wimbish, Keith LeBlanc and Brian Eno. The result feels contemporary yet unmistakably Sherwood, responding to "contemporary fuckries and impending madnesses" with both spiritual weight and flashes of psychedelic uplift. His RA Mix (yes, you read that right) arrives at a moment of renewal—RA.1001 is the first in a new era for the series. (After 1,000 editions of the RA Podcast, we're updating the name to better reflect what it's become.) Recorded at his Ramsgate studio, the 76-minute mix folds in cuts from The Collapse Of Everything alongside material from across the On-U Sound universe, plus collaborations with Panda Bear, Sonic Boom, Coldcut and Spoon. It's Sherwood doing what he's always done: stretching dub into endless new shapes. - Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? Working on tunes as usual. Right now, I've got my dear friend Doug Wimbish staying with me at my home studio in Ramsgate—he's doing gigs in Europe and we love hanging out together. Obviously doing a lot of promotion for my album, The Collapse Of Everything, because I want people to know it exists! As Chris Blackwell once said: "A terrible thing happens when you don't promote… Nothing!" It's an album I'm very proud of. I've got some gigs on the horizon as well, for the first time ever I'm going to be touring with my own live band. Instead of just doing a live dub show, I'm going to be on stage mixing the drums, percussion and live musicians with Alex White, who plays with Primal Scream, Fat White Family and Mark Bandola. We'll also have new visuals made by Peter Harris, so I can bring the new album to life and try to create a trippy, psychedelic live experience. Preparations are well underway. Where was the mix recorded? The mix was recorded in my Ramsgate studio. It's got some stuff from the new record, as well as a cross-section of productions or remixes I've done in the last few years—both for my own On-U Sound label and work with artists like Sonic Boom, Panda Bear, Coldcut and Spoon. What are you looking forward to in the near future? I'm looking forward to doing something new with my music and the live show, rather than just treading the old path. Apart from that, life's good at the moment. I feel very blessed with my family and friends and where I'm at. I'm quite happy. I'm trying to balance doing two or three days' work maximum in the studio, so that every time I go in there, I feel like I'm going to do something special, not just go through the motions. I'm finding the balance between enjoying my life and the people around me, reading and studying, while working on projects that I'm really excited to go into the studio. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? Well, there's so many things today. Obviously the horrors all over the world: the terrible situations in Palestine, Yemen and so many other places; the overprinting of money; the abuse of the environment, the power in the hands of so few is just awful. But if I can shed some light on local issues here in the UK, the priority for me is unifying people at a time when people are so divided. I think we've got a real problem in this country at the moment. Young people have got a terrible deal, there's no youth clubs, nothing connecting them to each other. I think some initiatives that promote more social cohesion for youngsters and older folk would be what I would prioritise domestically, to reduce the division and xenophobia we're experiencing. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? All the early sound system clubs from my youth. There's a place called the Newlands Club in High Wycombe that had a big impact on my life. I saw both punk gigs and Jamaican sound systems, and in later years, I remember witnessing the advent of the acid house scene. There was also The Dungeons off the Lea Bridge Road in East London. I only went on a handful of occasions. I remember there was a backdrop of football violence in that era, but suddenly all the old football firms used to go to these dances and we were cuddling each other on ecstasy. It was a beautiful moment in time. Dub has always thrived on live sound systems, where space and bass hit differently. When you're recording a mix for headphones or clubs, how do you think about capturing that feeling? I don't use headphones during the production or mixing process, but I trust my mixes will sound as intended on them. It's important to hear what you recorded sounding right on headphones, in clubs, in the car, or even on a phone nowadays. I try often to make sounds leap out and entertain me and keep space in the productions to try and keep the mix uncluttered. Dub mixes have beautiful '"space,"' and on good mixes the listener can almost hear things that aren't there. Your RA Mix is coming out the same week as Notting Hill Carnival. How do you see carnival's place in the story of UK sound system culture? It's incredibly important. In the early days, it moved from its early Calypso origins of live music, to letting the sound men loose on the streets of London for the first time. Lots of folk got to experience the power of sound system culture for the first time at carnival, and a real sense of unity has developed. It's a very healthy scene, very multi-generational and one that gathers politicised, conscious and good souls. Your new album, The Collapse Of Everything, is psychedelic and expansive. Do you see that as connected to Notting Hill Carnival's tradition of immersive, all-consuming sound? I love carnival, and elements of it can definitely be very trippy. The great thing is everyone is experiencing a proper audio visual attack, with loud music, costumes, dancing and everything else. I have felt very "otherworldly" vibes from my first visits in the mid-'70s— spiritual, magic, shared experiences. With so many great sound systems, both static and moving around on the trucks, it's very special indeed. I'd love to microdose the crowd and play tracks from The Collapse of Everything there.
translation.blurb: Expansive dub vibrations from the On-U Sound maestro.
